questions.stem: An integer $a$ with $p \nmid a$ is a quadratic residue modulo $p$ if $x^2 \equiv a \pmod p$ has a solution. Which one of the following is a quadratic residue modulo $7$?
questions.explanation: Square each residue modulo $7$: $1^2 = 1$, $2^2 = 4$, $3^2 = 2$, and by symmetry the nonzero quadratic residues are $\lbrace 1, 2, 4 \rbrace$. Since $3^2 \equiv 2$, the value $2$ is a residue. The others, $3, 5, 6$, are non-residues (no square hits them). The trap is guessing without squaring; only $1, 2, 4$ arise as squares modulo $7$.
questions.source: in the style of Burton, Elementary Number Theory

questions.stem: How many nonzero quadratic residues are there modulo an odd prime $p$?
questions.explanation: The squaring map $x \mapsto x^2$ on the nonzero residues is two-to-one (since $x$ and $-x$ give the same square), so exactly half of the $p - 1$ nonzero residues are squares: $(p-1)/2$ of them. For $p = 7$ that is $3$, matching $\lbrace 1, 2, 4 \rbrace$. The value $p - 1$ counts all nonzero residues. The trap is forgetting the two-to-one pairing; only half are quadratic residues.
questions.source: in the style of Burton, Elementary Number Theory

questions.stem: The Legendre symbol $\left( \dfrac{a}{p} \right)$ equals $1$ if $a$ is a quadratic residue, $-1$ if it is a non-residue, and $0$ if $p \mid a$. What is $\left( \dfrac{2}{7} \right)$?
questions.explanation: Since $3^2 = 9 \equiv 2 \pmod 7$, the value $2$ is a quadratic residue mod $7$, so $\left( \tfrac{2}{7} \right) = 1$. By Euler's criterion this also equals $2^{(7-1)/2} = 2^3 = 8 \equiv 1 \pmod 7$, confirming it. The value $-1$ would mark $2$ as a non-residue. The trap is misremembering the residues; $2$ is a square modulo $7$, giving $+1$.
questions.source: in the style of Burton, Elementary Number Theory

questions.stem: Euler's criterion states $\left( \dfrac{a}{p} \right) \equiv a^{(p-1)/2} \pmod p$. Use it to evaluate $\left( \dfrac{3}{7} \right)$.
questions.explanation: Compute $3^{(7-1)/2} = 3^3 = 27 \equiv 6 \equiv -1 \pmod 7$. So $\left( \tfrac{3}{7} \right) = -1$, meaning $3$ is a non-residue modulo $7$. This agrees with the residue set $\lbrace 1, 2, 4 \rbrace$ not containing $3$. The value $1$ would require $3^3 \equiv 1$. The trap is mis-reducing $27$; it leaves remainder $6 \equiv -1$, so the symbol is $-1$.
questions.source: in the style of Burton, Elementary Number Theory

questions.stem: There is a rule that $\left( \dfrac{-1}{p} \right) = 1$ if $p \equiv 1 \pmod 4$ and $-1$ if $p \equiv 3 \pmod 4$. What is $\left( \dfrac{-1}{13} \right)$?
questions.explanation: Since $13 \equiv 1 \pmod 4$, the rule gives $\left( \tfrac{-1}{13} \right) = 1$, so $-1$ is a quadratic residue mod $13$. Indeed $5^2 = 25 \equiv -1 \pmod{13}$. The value $-1$ would apply to primes $\equiv 3 \pmod 4$. The trap is checking the wrong congruence class; $13$ leaves remainder $1$ on division by $4$, so $-1$ is a square.
questions.source: in the style of Burton, Elementary Number Theory

questions.stem: Using the rule for $\left( \dfrac{-1}{p} \right)$, evaluate $\left( \dfrac{-1}{7} \right)$.
questions.explanation: Since $7 \equiv 3 \pmod 4$, the rule gives $\left( \tfrac{-1}{7} \right) = -1$, so $-1$ is a non-residue mod $7$. Equivalently, $-1 \equiv 6$ is not in $\lbrace 1, 2, 4 \rbrace$. The value $1$ would need $7 \equiv 1 \pmod 4$. The trap is misclassifying $7$ mod $4$; it is $\equiv 3$, so $-1$ is not a square modulo $7$.
questions.source: in the style of Burton, Elementary Number Theory

questions.stem: There is a rule that $\left( \dfrac{2}{p} \right) = 1$ if $p \equiv \pm 1 \pmod 8$ and $-1$ if $p \equiv \pm 3 \pmod 8$. What is $\left( \dfrac{2}{11} \right)$?
questions.explanation: Since $11 \equiv 3 \pmod 8$, the rule gives $\left( \tfrac{2}{11} \right) = -1$, so $2$ is a non-residue mod $11$. Checking, the residues mod $11$ are $\lbrace 1, 3, 4, 5, 9 \rbrace$, which excludes $2$. The value $1$ would require $11 \equiv \pm 1 \pmod 8$. The trap is using the wrong class mod $8$; $11 \equiv 3$, so $2$ is a non-residue.
questions.source: in the style of Burton, Elementary Number Theory

questions.stem: Using the supplementary rule for $2$, evaluate $\left( \dfrac{2}{17} \right)$.
questions.explanation: Since $17 \equiv 1 \pmod 8$, the rule gives $\left( \tfrac{2}{17} \right) = 1$, so $2$ is a quadratic residue mod $17$. Indeed $6^2 = 36 \equiv 2 \pmod{17}$. The value $-1$ would apply to $p \equiv \pm 3 \pmod 8$. The trap is misreading $17$ mod $8$; it is $\equiv 1$, so $2$ is a square modulo $17$.
questions.source: in the style of Burton, Elementary Number Theory

questions.stem: The Legendre symbol is multiplicative: $\left( \dfrac{ab}{p} \right) = \left( \dfrac{a}{p} \right)\left( \dfrac{b}{p} \right)$. Given $\left( \dfrac{2}{7} \right) = 1$ and $\left( \dfrac{3}{7} \right) = -1$, what is $\left( \dfrac{6}{7} \right)$?
questions.explanation: By multiplicativity, $\left( \tfrac{6}{7} \right) = \left( \tfrac{2}{7} \right)\left( \tfrac{3}{7} \right) = 1 \times (-1) = -1$. So $6$ is a non-residue mod $7$, consistent with $6 \equiv -1$ being a non-residue since $7 \equiv 3 \pmod 4$. The value $1$ would arise from two residues or two non-residues. The trap is adding instead of multiplying the symbols; the product of $1$ and $-1$ is $-1$.
questions.source: in the style of Burton, Elementary Number Theory

questions.stem: The law of quadratic reciprocity says that for distinct odd primes $p, q$, $\left( \dfrac{p}{q} \right)\left( \dfrac{q}{p} \right) = (-1)^{\frac{p-1}{2} \cdot \frac{q-1}{2}}$. When is $\left( \dfrac{p}{q} \right) = \left( \dfrac{q}{p} \right)$?
options: When at least one of $p, q$ is $\equiv 1 \pmod 4$
questions.explanation: The sign $(-1)^{\frac{p-1}{2}\cdot\frac{q-1}{2}}$ is $+1$ unless both exponents are odd, which happens exactly when both $p \equiv 3$ and $q \equiv 3 \pmod 4$. So the two symbols are equal whenever at least one prime is $\equiv 1 \pmod 4$, and they differ in sign only when both are $\equiv 3$. The trap is thinking the symbols always match; they flip sign precisely in the both-$\equiv 3$ case.
questions.source: in the style of Burton, Elementary Number Theory

questions.stem: Use quadratic reciprocity to evaluate $\left( \dfrac{3}{11} \right)$.
questions.explanation: Both $3$ and $11$ are $\equiv 3 \pmod 4$, so reciprocity gives $\left( \tfrac{3}{11} \right) = -\left( \tfrac{11}{3} \right)$. Now $11 \equiv 2 \pmod 3$, so $\left( \tfrac{11}{3} \right) = \left( \tfrac{2}{3} \right) = -1$ (since $3 \equiv 3 \pmod 8$). Thus $\left( \tfrac{3}{11} \right) = -(-1) = 1$, and indeed $5^2 = 25 \equiv 3 \pmod{11}$. The trap is dropping the sign flip; both primes $\equiv 3 \pmod 4$ force the minus, ending at $+1$.
questions.source: in the style of Burton, Elementary Number Theory

questions.stem: Use quadratic reciprocity to evaluate $\left( \dfrac{7}{11} \right)$.
questions.explanation: Both $7$ and $11$ are $\equiv 3 \pmod 4$, so $\left( \tfrac{7}{11} \right) = -\left( \tfrac{11}{7} \right)$. Reduce $11 \equiv 4 \pmod 7$, so $\left( \tfrac{11}{7} \right) = \left( \tfrac{4}{7} \right) = 1$ (as $4 = 2^2$ is a perfect square). Hence $\left( \tfrac{7}{11} \right) = -1$, and indeed $7$ is among the non-residues $\lbrace 2, 6, 7, 8, 10 \rbrace$ mod $11$. The trap is forgetting the sign flip; the answer is $-1$.
questions.source: in the style of Burton, Elementary Number Theory

questions.stem: What is $\left( \dfrac{1}{p} \right)$ for any odd prime $p$?
questions.explanation: Since $1 = 1^2$ is always a perfect square, $1$ is a quadratic residue modulo every prime, so $\left( \tfrac{1}{p} \right) = 1$. By Euler's criterion this is $1^{(p-1)/2} = 1$. The choice 'it depends' overlooks that $1$ is universally a square. The trap is overthinking; the Legendre symbol of $1$ is always $1$.
questions.source: in the style of Burton, Elementary Number Theory

questions.stem: The product of two quadratic non-residues modulo $p$ is:
questions.explanation: Using multiplicativity, if $\left( \tfrac{a}{p} \right) = \left( \tfrac{b}{p} \right) = -1$, then $\left( \tfrac{ab}{p} \right) = (-1)(-1) = 1$, so the product is a quadratic residue. For example, modulo $7$, both $3$ and $5$ are non-residues, and $3 \times 5 = 15 \equiv 1$, which is a residue. The trap is assuming non-residue times non-residue stays a non-residue; the signs multiply to $+1$, giving a residue.
questions.source: in the style of Burton, Elementary Number Theory

questions.stem: For which residues $a$ does the congruence $x^2 \equiv a \pmod p$ (with $p \nmid a$) have a solution?
questions.explanation: By definition, $x^2 \equiv a \pmod p$ is solvable precisely when $a$ is a quadratic residue, that is $\left( \tfrac{a}{p} \right) = 1$. When the symbol is $-1$, no square root exists. For instance $x^2 \equiv 2 \pmod 7$ solves as $x = 3$ or $4$, while $x^2 \equiv 3 \pmod 7$ has no solution. The trap is thinking every $a$ has a square root; solvability requires the Legendre symbol to be $+1$.
questions.source: in the style of Burton, Elementary Number Theory

questions.stem: Which one of the following is a quadratic residue modulo $11$?
questions.explanation: Square the residues modulo $11$: $1, 4, 9, 5, 3$ (from $1^2$ to $5^2$), so the residues are $\lbrace 1, 3, 4, 5, 9 \rbrace$. Since $5^2 = 25 \equiv 3$, the value $3$ is a quadratic residue. The others, $2, 7, 10$, are non-residues. The trap is guessing; only $1, 3, 4, 5, 9$ are squares modulo $11$, and $3$ is the one listed.
questions.source: in the style of Burton, Elementary Number Theory

questions.stem: Using Euler's criterion, evaluate $\left( \dfrac{5}{7} \right)$ via $5^{3} \bmod 7$.
questions.explanation: Compute $5^{(7-1)/2} = 5^3 = 125 = 7 \times 17 + 6 \equiv 6 \equiv -1 \pmod 7$. So $\left( \tfrac{5}{7} \right) = -1$, marking $5$ as a non-residue mod $7$, consistent with the residue set $\lbrace 1, 2, 4 \rbrace$. The value $1$ would need $5^3 \equiv 1$. The trap is mis-reducing $125$; it leaves $6 \equiv -1$, so the symbol is $-1$.
questions.source: in the style of Burton, Elementary Number Theory

questions.stem: Evaluate the Legendre symbol $\left( \dfrac{10}{13} \right)$ using multiplicativity and the supplementary rules.
questions.explanation: Factor $10 = 2 \times 5$, so $\left( \tfrac{10}{13} \right) = \left( \tfrac{2}{13} \right)\left( \tfrac{5}{13} \right)$. Since $13 \equiv 5 \pmod 8$, $\left( \tfrac{2}{13} \right) = -1$. For $\left( \tfrac{5}{13} \right)$, as $5 \equiv 1 \pmod 4$, reciprocity gives $\left( \tfrac{5}{13} \right) = \left( \tfrac{13}{5} \right) = \left( \tfrac{3}{5} \right) = -1$. The product is $(-1)(-1) = 1$, and indeed $6^2 = 36 \equiv 10 \pmod{13}$. The trap is mishandling one factor; both are $-1$, so the answer is $+1$.
questions.source: in the style of Burton, Elementary Number Theory

questions.stem: What is $\left( \dfrac{a}{p} \right)$ when $p \mid a$?
questions.explanation: By definition the Legendre symbol is $0$ when $p \mid a$, since then $a \equiv 0$ and $x^2 \equiv 0$ has only the trivial root $x \equiv 0$, not a genuine residue or non-residue. For example $\left( \tfrac{14}{7} \right) = 0$. The trap is treating $0 \bmod p$ as a residue worth $+1$; the symbol is defined to be $0$ in this case.
questions.source: in the style of Burton, Elementary Number Theory

questions.stem: How many quadratic residues (counting the nonzero ones) are there modulo $13$?
questions.explanation: For an odd prime $p$, there are $(p-1)/2$ nonzero quadratic residues. With $p = 13$, that is $(13-1)/2 = 6$. Squaring confirms it: $\lbrace 1, 4, 9, 3, 12, 10 \rbrace$ has six elements. The value $12 = p - 1$ counts all nonzero residues. The trap is taking all units; exactly half, namely $6$, are squares modulo $13$.
questions.source: in the style of Burton, Elementary Number Theory

questions.stem: Using Euler's criterion, evaluate $\left( \dfrac{3}{5} \right)$ via $3^{2} \bmod 5$.
questions.explanation: Compute $3^{(5-1)/2} = 3^2 = 9 \equiv 4 \equiv -1 \pmod 5$. So $\left( \tfrac{3}{5} \right) = -1$, marking $3$ as a non-residue mod $5$; the residues are $\lbrace 1, 4 \rbrace$. The value $1$ would need $3^2 \equiv 1$. The trap is misreducing $9$; it is $\equiv 4 \equiv -1 \pmod 5$, so the symbol is $-1$.
questions.source: in the style of Burton, Elementary Number Theory

questions.stem: For an odd prime $p$, the value of $a^{(p-1)/2} \bmod p$ (with $p \nmid a$) is always one of:
questions.explanation: By Euler's criterion, $a^{(p-1)/2} \equiv \left( \tfrac{a}{p} \right) \pmod p$, and the Legendre symbol is $+1$ or $-1$ for $p \nmid a$. This follows because $\left( a^{(p-1)/2} \right)^2 = a^{p-1} \equiv 1$, so $a^{(p-1)/2}$ is a square root of $1$, namely $\pm 1$. The trap is expecting an arbitrary residue; the half-power always lands on $1$ or $-1$.
questions.source: in the style of Burton, Elementary Number Theory

questions.stem: Use the supplementary rule to evaluate $\left( \dfrac{2}{23} \right)$.
questions.explanation: Since $23 \equiv 7 \equiv -1 \pmod 8$, the rule $\left( \tfrac{2}{p} \right) = 1$ for $p \equiv \pm 1 \pmod 8$ gives $\left( \tfrac{2}{23} \right) = 1$, so $2$ is a quadratic residue mod $23$ (indeed $5^2 = 25 \equiv 2$). The value $-1$ would apply to $p \equiv \pm 3 \pmod 8$. The trap is misreading $23$ mod $8$; it is $\equiv -1$, so $2$ is a residue.
questions.source: in the style of Burton, Elementary Number Theory

questions.stem: Find all nonzero quadratic residues modulo $7$ by squaring, and count them.
questions.solution: Squaring the residues gives $1^2 = 1$, $2^2 = 4$, and $3^2 = 9 \equiv$ {{1}} modulo $7$; the residues $4, 5, 6$ repeat these by symmetry. So the distinct nonzero quadratic residues modulo $7$ number {{2}}.
questions.explanation: $3^2 = 9 \equiv 2 \pmod 7$, so the residues are $\lbrace 1, 2, 4 \rbrace$, which is $(7-1)/2 = 3$ values.
questions.source: in the style of Burton, Elementary Number Theory

questions.stem: Use Euler's criterion to evaluate $\left( \dfrac{3}{7} \right)$ by computing $3^{(7-1)/2} \bmod 7$.
questions.solution: The exponent is $(7-1)/2 = 3$, so compute $3^3 = 27$. Reducing, $27 \bmod 7$ equals {{1}}. Since this represents $-1$ modulo $7$, the Legendre symbol $\left( \dfrac{3}{7} \right)$ equals {{2}}.
questions.explanation: $3^3 = 27 \equiv 6 \equiv -1 \pmod 7$, so $\left( \tfrac{3}{7} \right) = -1$ and $3$ is a non-residue.
questions.source: in the style of Burton, Elementary Number Theory

questions.stem: Apply the supplementary rules to evaluate $\left( \dfrac{-1}{11} \right)$ and $\left( \dfrac{2}{11} \right)$.
questions.solution: Since $11 \equiv 3 \pmod 4$, the symbol $\left( \dfrac{-1}{11} \right)$ equals {{1}}. Since $11 \equiv 3 \pmod 8$, the symbol $\left( \dfrac{2}{11} \right)$ equals {{2}}.
questions.explanation: As $11 \equiv 3 \pmod 4$, $\left( \tfrac{-1}{11} \right) = -1$. As $11 \equiv 3 \pmod 8$, $\left( \tfrac{2}{11} \right) = -1$. Both $-1$ and $2$ are non-residues mod $11$.
questions.source: in the style of Burton, Elementary Number Theory

questions.stem: Use quadratic reciprocity to evaluate $\left( \dfrac{5}{7} \right)$. (Note $5 \equiv 1 \pmod 4$.)
questions.solution: Because $5 \equiv 1 \pmod 4$, reciprocity gives $\left( \dfrac{5}{7} \right) = \left( \dfrac{7}{5} \right)$ with no sign change. Reducing $7 \equiv 2 \pmod 5$, we need $\left( \dfrac{2}{5} \right)$, which by the rule for $2$ (since $5 \equiv 5 \pmod 8$) equals {{1}}. Therefore $\left( \dfrac{5}{7} \right)$ equals {{2}}.
questions.explanation: Since $5 \equiv 5 \equiv -3 \pmod 8$, $\left( \tfrac{2}{5} \right) = -1$, so $\left( \tfrac{5}{7} \right) = -1$. This matches $5$ being a non-residue mod $7$.
questions.source: in the style of Burton, Elementary Number Theory

questions.stem: Decide whether $x^2 \equiv 5 \pmod{11}$ is solvable, using the residues modulo $11$.
questions.solution: The quadratic residues modulo $11$ are $\lbrace 1, 3, 4, 5, 9 \rbrace$. Since $5$ appears in this set, the Legendre symbol $\left( \dfrac{5}{11} \right)$ equals {{1}}, and so the congruence {{2}} a solution.
questions.explanation: Since $5$ is a quadratic residue mod $11$ ($4^2 = 16 \equiv 5$), $\left( \tfrac{5}{11} \right) = 1$ and the congruence has a solution, namely $x \equiv 4$ or $x \equiv 7$.
questions.source: in the style of Burton, Elementary Number Theory

questions.stem: Evaluate $\left( \dfrac{6}{7} \right)$ using the multiplicativity of the Legendre symbol.
questions.solution: Factor $6 = 2 \times 3$, so $\left( \dfrac{6}{7} \right) = \left( \dfrac{2}{7} \right)\left( \dfrac{3}{7} \right)$. Here $\left( \dfrac{2}{7} \right) = 1$ and $\left( \dfrac{3}{7} \right) = -1$, so their product, the value of $\left( \dfrac{6}{7} \right)$, is {{1}}. This means $6$ {{2}} a quadratic residue modulo $7$.
questions.explanation: $\left( \tfrac{6}{7} \right) = (1)(-1) = -1$, so $6$ is not a quadratic residue mod $7$. The residues are $\lbrace 1, 2, 4 \rbrace$, excluding $6$.
questions.source: in the style of Burton, Elementary Number Theory
